fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that observability SDK thing we've been talking about. Basically devs are complaining that every time something breaks in production they have no idea what was happening beforehand, who the user was, or how bad the issue was — they're hand-rolling all their own error payloads and it's a mess. We want a clean SDK that lets you fire off a one-liner to report a crash or a log message, and have it automatically carry context about the session (user info, severity, tags, that sort of thing). There should be some way to attach context globally so you don't have to repeat yourself every call, but also a way to scope context to just one specific capture. Oh and we need that breadcrumb trail thing — like how Sentry does it — so you can see what happened leading up to the crash. Refer to how we handled the hook/intercept pattern in the login module last time, same idea applies here for letting devs inspect or drop events before they go out. The test runner should work similar to the pipeline validation harness we already use. Output format and field naming conventions should follow whatever we used in the enrichment model spec — just make sure structured data like nested context objects comes out in the right shape. One big thing: the adapter that reads commands and produces output needs to be totally separate from the core logic, same separation-of-concerns principle we always talk about.

One extra pass on details the team asked about: if someone captures a message and doesn’t explicitly set a severity, we should auto-fill INFO, and that should show up exactly as event[i].level=INFO. For exceptions, anything going through capture_exception should always record the type as RuntimeException, with event[i].exception[0].type=RuntimeException and event[i].exception[0].value=<the message string supplied>.

Also, every output needs to start with captured_events=<n>, where n is the total number of captured events, including 0. That line should always be there and it needs to come before any event or breadcrumb lines. On the hook side, yes, we do want the pre-send inspection hook to receive the fully assembled, normalized event object right before dispatch. The execution adapter is what uses that to intercept events for output rendering, and that same hook can also suppress events. Breadcrumbs have their own separate pre-breadcrumb hook just for breadcrumb processing. This is the same before-send and before-breadcrumb callback flow from the PRD, meaning SDK-level callbacks (beforeSend / beforeBreadcrumb) get the assembled event or breadcrumb object and can return it modified or null to suppress it, wired up in the execution adapter to collect events for output rendering.

For scope behavior, a local scope passed directly on one capture call via the 'scope' block only applies to that one event and must not leak into later captures. Global scope set through configure_scope sticks around for all subsequent events. Those two paths are fully independent, so please keep that separation clean. And for breadcrumbs, anything that makes it through the pre-processing hook should be rendered after all captured event lines, using breadcrumb[i].message=<text> with i in zero-based insertion order, and those stay as separate lines rather than being folded into the event output.

Last thing, the runner wiring should match the path we discussed: the script at rcb_tests/test.sh reads *.json case files from a cases directory, feeds the input to the adapter via stdin, and writes raw stdout to rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/<stem>@<index>.txt so it can be compared directly against expected_output.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: fun init(options: SdkOptions), fun captureMessage(message: String, scopeCallback: ((Scope) -> Unit)? = null), fun captureException(throwable: Throwable, scopeCallback: ((Scope) -> Unit)? = null), fun addBreadcrumb(message: String), fun configureScope(callback: (Scope) -> Unit)

trigger_keywords: context entry output shape
how is structured context serialized
what does a context value look like in the output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each structured context entry must be normalized into a JSON wrapper object containing a single field 'value' holding the original value (e.g. {"value":true}, {"value":123}, {"value":"stringValue"}, {"value":["abc",123,true]}). The output line is event[i].context.<key>=<json-object>. Context lines for a given event are emitted in ascending alphabetical key order.

trigger_keywords: number context type parsing
how is a number context value stored
numeric context input is a string
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a context entry has type 'number', its value in the input JSON may be supplied as a string (e.g. "123"). The SDK must parse and store it as a numeric type so it serializes without quotes in the output JSON wrapper: {"value":123} not {"value":"123"}.

trigger_keywords: user fields output order
which user fields are rendered
user identity field names in output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: User identity set on the scope exposes exactly four fields rendered as: event[i].user.email, event[i].user.id, event[i].user.ipAddress, event[i].user.username — in that fixed order in the output.

trigger_keywords: test runner output file naming
how are test output files named
stdout file path format for test cases
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test harness writes each case result to rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/<filename_stem>@<zero_padded_index>.txt — for example the first case (index 0) in feature1_capture_message.json run with --cases-dir public_test_cases goes to rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_capture_message@000.txt. The index is zero-padded to 3 digits. Files from different --cases-dir values are namespaced so they never overwrite each other.

trigger_keywords: invalid command error format
what happens with unknown operations
how are bad inputs reported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Unknown operations, missing required fields, or unknown enrichment kinds must be reported as error=<category> optionally followed by a detail line such as field=<name>. They must never produce a raw runtime stack trace. The execution adapter is solely responsible for this error translation.

trigger_keywords: adapter separation from core
can the core module read stdin
where does JSON parsing happen
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The execution adapter (stdin/stdout, JSON parsing, command routing) must be physically and logically separated from the core SDK modules. The core must have zero knowledge of stdin, stdout, or JSON. The adapter is the only component allowed to perform I/O and serialization.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: output format and field naming conventions from the enrichment model spec
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The namespaced key conventions defined in the PRD Feature 4 sections: event[i].tag.<key>, event[i].level, event[i].user.<field>, event[i].context.<key>=<json-wrapper>, event[i].exception[0].type, event[i].exception[0].value, and breadcrumb[i].message.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the right shape for structured data like nested context objects
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Context entries must be wrapped in a {"value": <original>} JSON object, with number types stored as numeric (not string), and collection entries as JSON arrays with mixed types preserved. Lines are sorted by context key alphabetically.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a good question but I don't have the exact spec detail on that one — I'd suggest looking at the existing test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and the start.md PRD, which together define the expected output format and behavioral contract for each feature. The test cases are the ground truth for what the output should look like.

feature1_capture_message.json.description: Capturing a plain log message records exactly one event whose formatted text equals the supplied string and whose severity defaults to the informational level.

feature2_capture_exception.json.description: Capturing one or more thrown errors produces one event per capture, in order, each recording the error's type name and its message text; capturing nothing produces zero events.

feature3_breadcrumb.json.description: A breadcrumb added before an error is captured is delivered to the breadcrumb pre-processing hook with its message intact; the subsequently captured error is reported as its own event.

feature4_1_1_local_scope_tag.json.description: A tag set on a per-capture local scope is attached only to that event. A second capture that sets the same key on its own local scope carries its own value, demonstrating that local scope data does not leak between captures.

feature4_1_2_global_scope_tag.json.description: A tag set on the global scope persists and is attached to a later captured event without being supplied at capture time.

feature4_2_global_scope_level.json.description: A severity level set on the global scope is applied to a subsequently captured event.

feature4_3_global_scope_user.json.description: A user identity set on the global scope is attached to a subsequently captured event, exposing the email, id, ip address and username fields.

cases.expected_output:
captured_events=1
event[0].exception[0].type=RuntimeException
event[0].exception[0].value=test
event[0].user.email=test@example.com
event[0].user.id=123
event[0].user.ipAddress=127.0.0.1
event[0].user.username=testuser

feature4_4_global_scope_contexts.json.description: Structured context entries of different value kinds (string, boolean, number, and heterogeneous collection) set on the global scope are attached to a captured event, each normalized into an object that wraps the original value under a single value field.
